entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - CITS Extension - Generate Technical Specifications Directly from the iFlow Designer
entry.content:
Update — CITS v1.1.1 Final Release - 18/07/2026

CITS has reached its final version.

The original objective remains unchanged: reduce repetitive documentation effort and generate a consistent technical specification baseline directly from a saved SAP Cloud Integration iFlow. However, the extension has evolved significantly since the initial publication of this article.

What changed in v1.1.1

  • The Generate Specification action is now integrated directly into the SAP Cloud Integration iFlow editor.
  • The action remains available in both Edit and Save modes.
  • CITS works independently and does not require SAP CPI Helper.
  • The configuration interface was redesigned with clearer sections and guided options.
  • Optional dynamic headers and footers can include the iFlow name, environment, document version, confidentiality classification, generation date, and Word page numbering.
  • Users can choose between DOC and DOCX output.
  • A document preview is available before export.
  • Document Control was moved to the cover page.
  • iFlow details and the direct SAP Integration Suite link are presented in a dedicated section.
  • Script, XSLT, Message Mapping, schema, and resource information was consolidated into a clearer resource inventory with Used and Not Referenced status.
  • Interface and adapter details include the technical information exposed by the saved design-time model, including ProcessDirect addresses when available.
  • The generated document was simplified to remove sections that did not provide enough reliable value.

CITS continues to process the iFlow information locally in the browser and does not use an external backend.

Version 1.1.1 is the final public release. No further feature updates or new versions are planned.

The remainder of this article documents the original motivation, architecture, capabilities, and intended usage of CITS. Some screenshots and references to the original floating button and DOC-only export represent earlier versions of the extension. The current release uses the integrated Generate Specification action and supports both DOC and DOCX.

Documentation should not take longer than the integration development itself. 

Introducing CITS: A Free Technical Specification Generator for SAP Cloud Integration

Anyone who has worked with SAP Cloud Integration for a while knows the documentation gap.

You spend time building a complex iFlow, validating router conditions, Content Modifiers, scripts, mappings, adapters, credentials, and resources. But then comes the repetitive part: documenting everything.

In real-world projects, this usually means taking screenshots, opening multiple steps manually, listing headers and properties, checking mapping logic, copying adapter details, and trying to keep a Word document aligned with the latest iFlow version.

This process is time-consuming, error-prone, and often becomes outdated as soon as the iFlow changes.

To help with this, I created CITS — Cloud Integration Technical Specification Generator.

I initially built it for my own delivery work: to improve the quality, consistency, professionalism, and technical depth of my documentation, while making it easier to extend or reuse local project templates.

Now I am sharing it with the SAP Community as a 100% free productivity browser extension.

CITS generates technical specifications directly from SAP Cloud Integration iFlows.

What CITS Does

CITS goes beyond simple screenshots. It analyzes the saved design-time model and generates a structured technical documentation baseline.

The generated specification can include:

  • Architecture overview diagram
  • Process summary
  • Sender and receiver/interface details
  • Adapter and security configuration summary
  • Content Modifier details
  • Headers and properties inventory
  • Router and routing condition summary
  • Script and mapping resources
  • Message Mapping field inventory
  • Resource usage inventory
  • Review notes and assumptions

The generated file is a Word-compatible .DOC document, so it can be reviewed, copied, adjusted, and shared according to each project’s documentation process.

Core Capabilities

Architecture Overview

CITS generates a high-level architecture diagram from the iFlow model, helping reviewers quickly understand the trigger or sender, main process, local processes, outbound calls, receiver systems, adapters, and protocols.

Process and Mapping Inventory

CITS extracts relevant technical details from Content Modifiers and Message Mappings, including headers, properties, source fields, target fields, direct mappings, standard functions, and UDF/custom functions.

Resource Usage Check

Large projects often contain old Groovy scripts, XSLT files, schemas, or mappings that nobody wants to delete because nobody is sure whether they are still used.

CITS includes a resource inventory to help identify resources that are used, referenced, or potentially unused.

Adapter and Security Summary

CITS summarizes technical references such as endpoints, queues, topics, credential aliases, certificate aliases, authentication methods, and connectivity details when they are available in the design-time model.

CITS floating button inside the SAP Cloud Integration iFlow designer.

How to Install and Use CITS

Installation is straightforward.

  1. Open Google Chrome.
  2. Go to the Chrome Web Store.
  3. Search for CITS.
  4. Click Add to Chrome and confirm the installation.
  5. Open your SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  6. Go to an integration package and open an iFlow.

Direct link: SAP CITS

When CITS detects an iFlow designer page, the floating action button appears inside the designer.

🔒Security & Transparency Note: Because CITS was just launched and my developer profile is new, Google Chrome's "Enhanced Safe Browsing" might display a temporary “Proceed with caution / Not considered trusted” popup when you click install.

As an Independent Architecture Researcher, I designed CITS with a 100% local processing architecture. The extension does not use any backend servers, does not expose any credential and transmits absolutely zero data outside your browser window. You can safely click "Continue installation". 

Generating Your First Document in 2 clicks

  1. Open the iFlow.
  2. Save the iFlow if you recently changed it.
  3. Click the CITS button.
  4. Refresh the captured model.
  5. Preview the generated technical specification.
  6. Download the Word-compatible .DOC file.
  7. Adjust the document if your company has an official template.

CITS does not require the iFlow to be deployed. It works from the saved design-time model.

Optional project metadata can be added before generating the technical specification.

iFlow Architecture in the Document

The architecture overview is designed to provide a clean technical view for documentation and peer review.

It is not intended to replace the SAP Cloud Integration graphical editor. Its purpose is to make the generated document easier to understand and review.

Real Iflow:

Canvas of the diagram in the document

 

Generated .DOC technical specification with an embedded iFlow architecture canvas.

Message Mapping Documentation

For Message Mapping scenarios, CITS can document the mapping structure in a searchable format.

The generated specification can include:

  • Source resource
  • Target resource
  • Source fields
  • Target fields
  • Direct mappings
  • Standard functions
  • UDF/custom functions
  • Mapping inventory table

This helps reduce the need to open the Message Mapping editor just to understand basic field-to-field transformations.

Message Mapping documentation with source fields, transformations, target fields, and UDF/standard function visibility.

Template Approach

CITS currently generates one standard technical specification template.

This is intentional.

Every company has its own documentation governance, approval sections, naming conventions, branding, and compliance wording. CITS is designed to generate a strong technical baseline, not to replace internal governance.

Recommended usage:

  1. Generate the CITS technical specification.
  2. Review the technical content.
  3. Copy relevant sections into the official company template if needed.
  4. Add project-specific approvals, governance notes, or business context.

Privacy and Security

CITS runs in the browser.

There is no external backend used to generate the document. The generated .DOC file is created locally from the iFlow information available in the browser session.

As always, companies should review browser extension usage according to their own internal security and compliance policies.

Limitations

CITS is a productivity tool, not an official SAP product.

A few important points:

  • Unsaved iFlow changes may not be reflected.
  • The iFlow should be saved before generating the document.
  • The generated specification should still be reviewed by an integration developer or architect.
  • Some adapter details depend on what SAP Cloud Integration exposes in the design-time model.
  • Company-specific governance sections may need to be added manually.

Final Thoughts

CITS was created to reduce repetitive technical documentation work for SAP Cloud Integration projects.

It does not replace solution design, functional specification, testing evidence, governance, or peer review. It helps generate a consistent technical baseline faster.

The objective is simple:

Less manual documentation effort. More consistent technical review.

I hope this helps SAP Integration developers, consultants, architects, and project teams dealing with recurring documentation requirements.

Feedback is welcome.

Related Independent Solo Research Deliverables and SAP SCN Community Contributions

For readers interested in the broader research path behind CITS, these are some of the topics I have been publishing about since January 2026:

To be completely honest, I am well aware that dense topics focused on deep architecture and real governance typically attract a low level of immediate digital engagement. The reason is simple: the content I publish requires a foundational level of structural abstraction and engineering mindset that goes far beyond the operational, "click-and-configure" routine most of the market is used to consuming.

However, vanity metrics do not dictate the intrinsic value of what is engineered. If any of the topics listed below has helped you view the integration ecosystem through a more mature lens—even if invisibly, without a single "like" or public reaction—then my objective has been completely achieved.

The year 2026 has been a period of intensive, solo, and entirely independent technical production. My sole motivation remains to deliver both intellectual frameworks and practical tools to elevate the technical baseline of integration developers within the SAP SCN Community, helping anyone who has the drive to break out of the comfort zone.

Beyond SAP CPI "Expert label" - Motivational journey

  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP APIM Part I
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP EM/AEM Part II
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP IA - Part III
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant - Part IV

Architecture and Governance

  • SAP CPI - Smart JMS Retry with Exception-Based Routing in SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
  • SAP BTP - Integration Suite: Clean Architecture vs The Frankenstein Landscape

EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter full open source - License Apache 2.0

Step by step how to create a custom adapter using SAP ADK for SAP Cloud Integration, in this case the adapter is for KAFKA integration

  • SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
  • SAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
  • SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA - Validated Runtime - Part III
  • SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA v1.1.0 - Validated Runtime - Part IV

Github: EventSmartKafka - Custom Resources full open code and .esa for download 

SDIA - Applied in SAP - API Management ( GDCR + DDCR ) and SAP Cloud Integration ( ODCP )

  • SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice
  • SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

Architectural decision DEIP and Governance Architecture SDIA - full open source - License CC BY 4.0 Internacional

Github: All tests of GDCR + DDCR using SDIA 

Site: www.domain-intent.com

DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern - https://zenodo.org/records/19825678
└── Decision Pipeline
        → Intent
        → Interaction Type
        → Quality Profile
        → Integration Capability
        → Protocol Profile
        → Platform Binding
        └── SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture - https://zenodo.org/records/19788232
                 ├── GDCR — Gateway Domain-Centric Routing - https://zenodo.org/records/19711693
                  |         └── SAP BTP API Management
                 ├── DDCR — Domain Driven-Centric Router - https://zenodo.org/records/19820524
                  |        └── JavaScript Policy plus Key Value Mapping
                 ├── ODCP — Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern - https://zenodo.org/records/19786661
                  |         └── SAP BTP Cloud Integration
                 ├── EDCP — Event Domain-Centric Pattern - https://zenodo.org/records/19727832
                  |          └── SAP BTP EM/AEM
                 └── DDCP — Data Domain-Centric Pattern - https://zenodo.org/records/19730519
└── Execution Platforms

SAP BTP · AWS · Azure · Kong · Kafka · IoT · AI/LLM · etc.

entry.title: Turn SAP APIs into MCP Tools for AI Agents using SAP Integration Suite Free Trial
entry.content:
The MCP Capability for SAP Integration Suite is finally here, and everyone can try it now!

This last week I've been playing around with the new MCP Server artifact in SAP Integration Suite and created an end-to-end use case that I think is worth sharing with the community.

The idea is simple: take an official SAP API from the Business Accelerator Hub (in this example we will use the Business Partner (A2X) API for S/4HANA Public Cloud, as it has a public sandbox that we can use without the need to have a S/4HANA system), expose it through Integration Suite as an MCP server, and connect it to any MCP-compatible AI client so you can query business data using natural language.  All of this on a free trial account (you can get one here).

Before we dive deep into development I recommend checking this blog to get more details on what is a MCP Server, MCP Tool, and MCP Gateway and how they will work together in SAP Integration Suite to become the endorsed pathway for SAP API access: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250

Currently SAP Integration Suite supports 3 source types:

  • API – Create an MCP Server from an existing API artifact deployed on Integration Cell.
  • HTTP Endpoint with OpenAPI Specification – Create an MCP Server by importing an OpenAPI specification and exposing operations from any HTTP endpoint.
  • RFC – Create an MCP Server from SAP RFC-enabled function modules by exposing selected RFC operations as MCP Tools.

In this blog, we will use the API option (we will start by creating an API artifact, where we can add security policies, and only after we will create the MCP Server that will expose it). 

What you need before we start:

  • A BTP trial account (free at https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/trial.html)
  • Create a subaccount (the MCP capability is available in both USA and Singapore regions)
  • Create an instance of SAP Integration Suite with the trial plan
  • Activate the Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities in SAP IS (and assign the required roles - PI_Integration_Developer role collection - to your user in the BTP Cockpit)
  • Enable the new Integration Cell runtime (go to Settings -> Runtimes)
  • A SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance with plan integration-flow created in your Cloud Foundry space, with a service key. This gives you the OAuth credentials (clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl) needed to call the MCP server endpoint.
  • Any MCP-compatible AI client (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, or any agent framework supporting MCP)
  • The Business Partner API spec JSON downloaded from SAP Business Accelerator Hub, plus your API key from there

The architecture


The AI client connects to the MCP Server over streamable HTTP, authenticating with an OAuth Bearer token from SAP BTP.
The MCP Server exposes the Business Partner OData operations as tools that the AI model can discover and call.
When a tool is invoked, the MCP Server forwards the request to the API Artifact, which acts as a proxy to the SAP sandbox.
A Content Modifier policy on the API Artifact injects the Business Accelerator Hub API key before hitting the backend — so the AI client only ever needs the OAuth token, no backend credentials.

Note: The "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" setting on the API Artifact is what makes the internal call work — without it, the API Artifact will reject the request from the MCP Server as unauthorized. Enabling this option delegates all authorization to the upstream API. The MCP Server bypasses its own checks, which avoids redundant policy evaluations and maintains a single, unified security model.

Step 1: Create the API Artifact

Go to your Package → Add → API.
Select Integration Cell as the Runtime profile
Choose URL or Specification as the method
Select Upload and then:

  • upload the Business Partner JSON spec downloaded from the Business Accelerator Hub
  • set the URL to https://sandbox.api.sap.com
  • service type ODATA
  • base path /business-partner-api

Click on Add and Open in API Designer.

In the Policies tab, add a Content Modifier after the Authorization policy. Under Message Header, add:

  • Action: Create
  • Name: APIKey
  • Source Type: Constant
  • Source Value: <your-hub.sap.com-API-key>

In the Authorization policy, check "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" — this tells the API artifact not to re-validate auth when the MCP server calls it internally.

Set the Scope to ESBMessaging.send(alternatively you could add the API.invoke role in the Roles field when creating the Process Integration Runtime service instance and use it instead of the ESBMessaging.send; the ESBMessaging.send is just the default one there, what is important is that it matches).

In Target Endpoint, set the full backend URL: https://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER

Deploy.

Step 2: Create the MCP Server

Go back to your Package and click on Add → MCP Server → Source Type: API (from a deployed API artifact on Integration Cell):

  • Pick your BusinessPartner API artifact
  • Give it a Name
  • Set MCP Path to /business-partner

Then on the Tools step select the operations you want — select just the GET /A_BusinessPartner if you want to keep it simple. You will be able to add more tools and edit their details after if you want.

In the Authorization policy, change the scope to match what the integration-flow service key produces: ESBMessaging.send (or API.invoke if you set in when creating the Process Integration Runtime service instance)

Deploy.

Note: An MCP Server created from an API automatically inherits the source API's authentication policy. This maintains a consistent security model and eliminates the need for manual configuration. Because these settings are inherited, they are read-only and cannot be edited directly within the MCP Server. To update the authentication mechanism, you must modify it on the source API and then synchronize the MCP Server to pull in the changes.

Step 3: Get credentials

An MCP Server can be called using credentials from either a Process Integration Runtime (PIR) service instance or a Developer Hub subscription. In this post we will use the PIR approach.

In BTP Cockpit → Cloud Foundry → your space → Service Instances → find the SAP Process Integration Runtime instance with plan integration-flow → create a Service Key. You'll get a clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl.

Fetch a token:

curl -X POST "<tokenurl>/oauth/token" \
  -u "<clientid>:<clientsecret>" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials"

Step 4: Connect your AI client

You can find you MCP server endpoint in the monitoring area of SAP Integration Suite or in the general information of your MCP Server artifact:

https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/business-partner

The transport is streamable HTTP (not SSE). Pass the OAuth token as a Bearer header in the Authorization field of your MCP client configuration. The exact way to do this depends on your client — most have an MCP server settings file where you can set the URL, transport type, and custom headers.

Once it's working, you can ask your AI assistant things like "list the top 5 business partners" and it calls the MCP tool, hits the SAP sandbox, and returns relevant mock data.

I hope this use case can be a solid starting point for building MCP servers on SAP Integration Suite. You can use this capability to expose any API, from SAP or non-SAP. 

The core benefit of MCP: instead of developers hardcoding API calls or orchestration logic, AI agents analyze the user's intent to dynamically select and run the correct enterprise tools on the fly.

I hope I was able to make this blog easy to follow and I will be covering more advanced use cases in the next series of this post. Stay tuned!

entry.title: From SAP Integration Suite iFlow to MCP Tools: Exposing OData APIs to AI Agents
entry.content:
What is MCP and why does it matter for SAP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI agents discover and invoke tools at runtime. Instead of hardcoding API calls into your agent, you publish an MCP Server — a runtime endpoint that advertises a list of named, described tools. The agent reads those descriptions and decides autonomously which tool to call and with which parameters.

SAP Integration Suite now acts as an enterprise MCP Gateway: it hosts MCP Servers on the Integration Cell runtime, enforces OAuth2 authentication, rate limiting and audit logging, and lets you govern exactly which agents can call which tools.

In this post I'll show you how to go from zero to a working MCP Server in SAP Integration Suite (BTP Trial) — using the Northwind OData V4 API as the backend. Northwind uses the same OData V4 protocol as SAP S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, so everything here maps directly to real SAP backends.

The full end-to-end stack looks like this:

AI Agent (Claude Code)
   ↓  OAuth2 client_credentials  (scope: ESBMessaging.send)
MCP Server  —  Integration Cell runtime
   ↓  forwards Bearer token
Cloud Integration iFlow  (Northwind_API)
   ↓  CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery passthrough
Northwind OData V4   https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/

Prerequisites

  • SAP BTP trial account
  • SAP Integration Suite activated (trial plan) with Cloud Integration + API Management capabilities
  • Integration Cell runtime enabled and Active (Settings → Runtimes)
  • SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance — plan integration-flow — with a Service Key

Step 1 — Enable the Integration Cell runtime

Go to SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtime Profiles and activate both Cloud Integration and Integration Cell.

Then go to Settings → Runtimes and confirm both show status Active. The MCP Server artifact requires the Integration Cell runtime — it cannot be deployed to Cloud Integration.

Step 2 — Create an Integration Package

Go to Design → Integrations and APIs → Create and create a new package. I named mine MCP_TEST_IS. All artifacts for this PoC live here: the iFlow and the MCP Server.

 

Step 3 — Build the iFlow

Inside the package, create a new Integration Flow called Northwind_API. The flow is deliberately simple: a single HTTPS Sender → HTTP Receiver with no transformation steps.

HTTPS Sender

Field Value

Address/northwind/*
CSRF ProtectedNo
AuthorizationUser Role: ESBMessaging.send

The wildcard address /northwind/* means a single iFlow handles all three entity paths (/northwind/Employees, /northwind/Customers, /northwind/Orders). SAP CPI automatically captures the dynamic segment in the Camel header CamelHttpPath, and the full OData query string in CamelHttpQuery.

HTTP Receiver

Field Value

Addresshttps://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/${header.CamelHttpPath}?${header.CamelHttpQuery}
Query(leave empty)
MethodGET
AuthenticationNone

Important: Put the full URL including the query string directly in the Address field. Do not use the separate Query field — it escapes $ signs and breaks all OData parameters.

 

No Content Modifier. No Groovy script. The two Camel headers are populated automatically by the runtime.

Deploy the iFlow to the Cloud Integration runtime and wait for status Started.

Step 4 — Create the OpenAPI spec

The MCP Server needs an OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec. Each operationId becomes a tool name that the AI agent can discover and call. The description fields are what the agent reads to understand when and how to use each tool — write them for the agent, not for a human developer.

Two fields in the spec are specific to SAP Integration Suite:

  • info.x-targetEndpoint — the Cloud Integration runtime URL the MCP Server will forward calls to
  • servers[0].url — the Integration Cell base URL (used by clients to reach the MCP Server itself)
{
  "openapi": "3.0.1",
  "info": {
    "title": "NorthWind API",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "x-targetEndpoint": "https://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http"
  },
  "servers": [{ "url": "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind" }],
  "paths": {
    "/northwind/Employees": {
      "get": {
        "operationId": "getEmployees",
        "summary": "Get Employees",
        "description": "Returns employees. Supports OData: $filter (e.g. City eq 'London'), $top, $select, $orderby",
        "parameters": [
          { "name": "$filter", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$top",    "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "integer" } },
          { "name": "$select", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$orderby","in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } }
        ],
        "responses": { "200": { "description": "List of employees" } }
      }
    },
    "/northwind/Customers": { "...": "same pattern with getCustomers" },
    "/northwind/Orders":    { "...": "same pattern with getOrders + $expand" }
  }
}

Step 5 — Create the MCP Server artifact

Inside the same package, add a new artifact of type MCP Server.

Field Value

Namenorthwind_MCP
MCP Path/northwind-mcp
Source TypeHTTP Endpoint + OpenAPI Spec
URLhttps://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http

Upload the OpenAPI spec in the spec editor. The MCP Server parses it and shows 3 tools:

Tool name Description

getEmployeesGet Employees — OData filter, top, select, orderby
getCustomersGet Customers — OData filter, top, select
getOrdersGet Orders — OData filter, top, select, expand

Step 6 — Configure Policies (critical)

Under the MCP Server policies, configure:

  • Authentication: OAuth2, pointing to your XSUAA token URL
  • Authorization: change the scope from API.invoke to ESBMessaging.send

⚠️This is the most common mistake. The default Authorization policy scope is API.invoke. But the SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. If you leave the default, every call returns 403 Forbidden.

Step 7 — Deploy

Deploy the MCP Server to the Integration Cell runtime. Wait for status Started.

 

Step 8 — Test with curl

Get a Bearer token:

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "https://<your-tenant>.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

List available tools:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'

Call a tool:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call",
    "params":{
      "name":"getEmployees",
      "arguments":{"$filter":"City eq '\''Seattle'\''"}
    }
  }'

Results: Nancy Davolio, Laura Callahan. ✅

Step 9 — Connect Claude Code as an AI agent

Register the MCP Server in Claude Code:

claude mcp add northwind \
  --transport http \
  --url "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>"

Now ask in natural language. A few examples from the live demo:

"Show me employees in Spain"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=Country eq 'Spain' → empty result. It then self-corrects, calls again with $select=EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,City,Country to list all employees, and responds: "There are no employees in Spain. The 9 employees are in USA and UK."

"Give me more details about Robert King"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=EmployeeID eq 7 and presents his full profile: Sales Representative, London, hired January 1994, reports to Steven Buchanan.

 

"Show me the orders Robert has handled"
Claude calls getOrders with the employee filter and summarises: 72 orders total, 3 pending, highest freight $830.75, countries served across USA, UK, Germany, France, Brazil, Austria, Mexico, Spain and more.

 

The agent chains tool calls autonomously. When Spain returned no results it self-corrected without being asked. This is the power of intent-shaped tools with rich descriptions: the AI decides how to query, not just what to call.

Key learnings

1. Scope: ESBMessaging.send — not API.invoke

The SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. The default Authorization policy uses API.invoke. Always change it or you'll get 403 on every call.

2. CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery — no Groovy needed

A wildcard Sender address (/northwind/*) plus ${header.CamelHttpPath} and ${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Receiver Address is enough to route all entities dynamically through a single iFlow. No Content Modifier, no Groovy script.

3. Query string goes in the Address field

The Receiver's separate Query field escapes $ signs and breaks OData parameters. Put the full URL including ?${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Address field.

4. Intent-shaped tools beat endpoint-shaped tools

Name tools after business operations, not HTTP endpoints. Write descriptions for the AI agent — tell it when to use the tool and what parameters make sense. The agent uses those descriptions to decide autonomously what to call and how.

5. x-targetEndpoint is the routing key

The info.x-targetEndpoint field in the OpenAPI spec is what the MCP Server uses at runtime to forward calls to the iFlow. It must point to the Cloud Integration runtime URL, not the Integration Cell URL.

What's next

In the next post I'll cover Approach B — API Proxy: same result but the MCP Server sits in front of an API Artifact (deployed to Integration Cell) instead of an iFlow. That adds a full governance layer — rate limiting, IP blocking, payload protection — without changing a line of iFlow logic. The key new concept there is Trust Upstream MCP Authorization, which lets the API Artifact accept calls forwarded by the MCP Server without re-validating the token.

entry.title: New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the July 2026 shipment
entry.content:
We have just shipped new versions of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages. We have implemented a couple of feature enhancements, mainly based on customer demand or stuff we missed out the last time. A major feature that we have shipped is around retry handling, actually the option to skip retry for exceptions where a retry does not make sense.

The following new versions have been shipped:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound version 1.0.8
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines version 1.0.5
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound version 1.0.4
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates version 1.2.0

And here the list of new features at a glance:

  • Skip Retry Handling: The retry behavior has been changed. For some errors where an automatic message retry would not make sense, the retry is skipped and the message is directly stored in the dead letter queue. For example, if a message mapping fails, it doesn't make sense to automatically retry until we reach the maximum number of retries. If we won't fix the mapping, the message will constantly fail. So, in this case, the retry is skipped and the failed message is directly stored in the dead letter queue. For the skip retry handling we do support three options to configure the skip retry behaviour: hard-coded (this is the default), lockup a value mapping that maps exception classes to a boolean true/false, and lookup a profile in the Partner Directory. In this context, we have shipped a value mapping as part of the templates package as well as a helper flow to sync the value mapping entries with the Partner Directory. For more details, see Skip Retry Handling documentation or check out this blog post.
  • Sender and receiver queue names shown in MPL: For all asynchronous pipeline flows, the sender and receiver queue names have been added to the custom header attributes so that you have full transparency about the JMS queues that the messages pass through.
  • Display original error message when max retry has been exceeded: For generic integration flows that may do a split like receiver determination, interface determination and the integrated messaging runtime, we have to do one more retry if a message fails and the maximum number of retries have been exceeded. Otherwise, we may create duplicates. So far, the original error message was lost after the additional retry. Now, you have the option to configure the OData API details of your tenant for the generic integration flows affected to fetch the original error message from the message processing log. See note in Standard Retry Handling documentation.
  • Restore payload, headers, attachments in case of retry: In case that an erroneous message is stored in the dead letter queue, the payload, all headers, and all attachments if any are restored to ensure that they are in the state that they had at the JMS queue inbound. Before, this was not always guaranteed.
  • Flexible landscape stages scenario coverage: In the generic XI inbound processing flow, flexible landscape stages is now also supported for the Point-to-Point bypass option. Somehow, we missed this out.
  • Response body nulled for XI inbound: For asynchronous messages using the generic XI inbound processing flow, the body of the response is nulled after having stored the message in the JMS queue. This was reported by a customer that faced issues in the sender system in case of large messages.
  • Allowed header list enhanced: The allowed header list of the generic XI inbound processing flow and the generic IDoc inbound processing flow has been enhanced based on customer demand.
  • XI/IDoc connection parameters via business system or configuration scenario in Partner Directory: For the generic XI outbound processing flow and the generic IDoc outbound processing flow, the option maintaining the connection parameters in the Partner Directory has been enhanced. The parameters can be now maintained as string parameters of a partner ID representing a business system. Furthermore, some connection parameters can be also maintained as string parameters of a partner ID representing a configuration scenario. So far, this was only supported via string parameters in the corresponding business system alias. See Generic Inbound and Outbound documentation.
  • XI delivery assurance settings revised: For the generic XI outbound processing flow, the delivery assurance settings to determine the XI Message ID have been revised to ensure that a proper Universally Unique Identifier (UUID) is passed to the receiver system. For this, the value of the UniqueID header is mapped to the header SapMessageIdEx and the XI adapter now uses Reuse instead of Map.
  • XI message ID from end to end: Furthermore, in case of an XI to XI scenario, you have the option to pass the sender XI message ID from end-to-end. See Pass XI message ID from end-to-end.
  • IDoc unique ID revised: For the generic IDoc outbound processing flow, the processing settings to determine a unique ID have been revised. For this, the value of the UniqueID header is mapped to the header SapMessageId and the IDoc adapter now uses Reuse instead of Map.
  • Display payload size: For the generic outbound processing flow, you have the option to measure and display the size of the original payload.

Please update the packages in your Design area to benefit from the changes. We recommend to update your dev and test landscape first and to test there before updating the content on your prod tenant. Though we run extensive regression tests and new feature tests before publishing a new version. We even have extended our regression tests by implementing further tests and checks such as checking the number of completed, failed, and escalated logs, including negative tests, restarting a failed message from the dead letter queue (by leveraging the new OData API for the JMS queues), comparing custom status, comparing custom header properties, comparing error messages, etc.

entry.title: Pass XI message ID from end-to-end
entry.content:
With the new version of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages, see New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the July 2026 shipment, we have enhanced the generic XI inbound processing flow as well as the generic XI outbound processing flow to be able to pass the XI message ID from end-to-end. This enhancement was actually added based on a customer requirement. For specific scenarios where both the sender and the receiver use the XI 3.0 protocol, the customer likes to keep the sender XI message ID the same like in the receiving system to be able to track the message from end-to-end.

You can enable the feature for a specific configuration scenario by maintaining the string parameter PassMessageIdFromE2E in the partner ID representing your configuration scenario in the Partner Directory.

We intentionally did not introduce this feature as a global setting because you need to use this setting with caution. Wrongly used, this may lead to data loss. For example, if you duplicate or split up your message in your scenario-specific outbound processing flow that calls the generic XI outbound flow and each split message is sent to the same receiver, only the first message will be processed in the receiver system, all other messages are seen as duplicates in the receiver system because they have the same XI message ID which was successfully processed before and hence they will be discarded leading to data loss. We have implemented a check which will only work when using an iterative splitter or a generic splitter in your scenario-specific outbound processing flow to split up the message. In this case, even if the parameter PassMessageIdFromE2E is set, this setting will be ignored and a new unique ID for each split message is generated. If you use whatever other way to split up the message, the check won't work. So, as said, use this with caution and enable it only for use cases where you are sure that this won't lead to any harm.

So, now let's check how this can be configured and how this behaves. I have setup a configuration scenario of pattern Content-Based Router. For one receiver, I run a one-to-many message mapping to split the message. For another receiver, I only pass one message to the receiver.

In case you are not familiar with scenarios using the generic XI outbound processing flow, check out this blog post Generic XI Outbound flow in Cloud Integration Pipeline. For a better understanding, I schematically show you the message flow:

Sender --via XI protocol--> Generic XI Inbound Processing flow --via JMS adapter--> First JMS Queue --via JMS adapter--> Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow --via JMS adapter--> Second JMS Queue --via JMS adapter--> Generic Outbound Processing  flow --via ProcessDirect--> Scenario-specific Outbound Processing flow --via ProcessDirect--> Generic XI Outbound Processing flow --via XI protocol--> Receiver

So, in the Generic XI Inbound Processing flow I fetch the XI message ID from the Sender, store it in a header and route this through all flows. The Generic XI Inbound Processing flow stores the message in the first JMS queue where it is picked up by the Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow. The Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow carries out the xpath conditions to determine the receiver/s and then stores the message in the second JMS queue for each receiver. In our case, we always have one receiver only at the same time. The Generic Outbound Processing flow picks up the message/s from the second JMS queue and now tries to deliver this to the receiver. It calls the Scenario-specific Outbound Processing flow via ProcessDirect where the actual mapping is carried out and eventually the XI connection parameters are set. The connection to the Receiver is then done via the Generic XI Outbound Processing flow which acts as a sort of channel. If configured, we use the XI message ID from the Sender to create the XI message ID at the Receiver.

So, the screenshot below shows the scenario-specific integration flow where we split up the message. As you can see, we run a one-to-many message mapping, the result is a multi message which is embedded in Messages nodes. We need to get rid of the additional nodes and split up the message into multiple messages. This we do using an iterative splitter. The iterative splitter by the way adds a specific header to the exchange which we pass to the next flow which is the generic XI outbound processing flow where we check if this header is present and if so discard the use of the sender XI message ID. Furthermore, in a content modifier we need to add the Camel split index to the unique ID header so that each split message has a unique ID.

And the following screenshot shows the scenario-specific integration flow with a simple 1:1 mapping resulting in one single message. In this case, we can keep the unique ID header unchanged. But it won't be used anyway because we will pass the XI message ID from the sender to the XI receiver adapter in the generic XI outbound processing flow anyway.

I like to enable the feature for my scenario, at least for those cases were I do not do a split. In the Partner Directory, I add a new string parameter with ID PassMessageIdFromE2E and value true to the partner ID representing my configuration scenario.

Now, I run some tests. First, let's see how the end-to-end XI message ID feature works. In this case, in the message monitor I click on the Application Message ID and this will then automatically filter all logs that belong to the same Application Message ID. Here, the Application Message ID is actually the sender XI message ID. So, you can see that both, the generic XI inbound processing flow as well as the generic XI outbound processing flow have the same XI message ID. I only mocked the sender and receiver but in a real scenario the corresponding messages in the sender and the receiver system would be the same.

For another test message that I sent, the split is carried out. Here, I now click on the Correlation ID to be able to see all logs of the same exchange. Since we did a split, you see two logs of the generic XI outbound processing flow. If I clicked on the Application Message ID, you would not see all logs because the generic XI outbound processing flows have a different XI message ID and hence Application Message ID. Here, the check popped in. Because we did a split in the flow before, the unique ID header is used to generate the XI message ID instead of using the sender XI message ID. You remember, the unique ID header was modified by adding the slit index, so this ensures that the XI message IDs of the split messages differ. So, in this case you can't track the XI message ID from end-to-end but at least we add the sender XI message ID to the custom header properties. If we identify a split, we store the sender XI message ID in the custom header ParentIdBeforeMessageSplit.

Hope this new feature is of value for you. As usually, feedback is highly appreciated.

entry.title: New Skip Retry Handling feature in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration with the July 2026 shipment
entry.content:
In some error cases, an automatic retry doesn’t help, it just adds load on the tenant runtime without fixing anything. For those cases, you can skip the standard retry handling and send the failed messages directly to the Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). That’s exactly the purpose of the new "Skip Retry Handling" feature in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration (July 2026 shipment). This blog covers when retries are skipped by default, and how you can adjust the behavior globally or per scenario.

How “Skip Retry Handling” Helps

  • The tenant stops wasting runtime on errors that will never succeed.
  • Failures land in the DLQ quickly, so you can actually see what broke instead of digging through retry noise.
  • You can leave the defaults alone, or tune the behavior globally or per scenario.

Where Retries Are Skipped by Default

For the integrated messaging runtime (including the Generic Receiver Determination and Interface Determination flows), retries are skipped and messages go straight to the DLQ in these cases:
  • Partner ID could not be determined.
  • Configuration option not supported.
  • Receiver could not be determined when “Receiver Not Found” is set to Error.
  • Receiver interface could not be determined.
For all other error cases (in these components), the standard retry handling applies.

Tuning Skip Retries in Generic Outbound Processing

For the Generic Outbound Processing flow, you can fine-tune how skip retry handling works. Two control levels exist:
  • Global settings (apply to all copied Generic Outbound flows—unless overridden)
  • Scenario-specific settings (per integration scenario via Partner Directory)
By default, the global skip is enabled:
  • Parameter: SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true

Configuration options

OptionHow to ActivateWhat it doesWhen to use?
Default (Hard-coded)SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = DefaultSkips retries only for Mapping errors (com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException)Good baseline if mapping errors are your main non-transient case
Global via Value MappingSkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping and deploy the “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration”Centralizes per-exception-class settings in a value mappingWhen you want one global control point for all scenarios
Global via Partner ID ProfileSkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory plus a Partner ID profileLets you define a skip-retry profile (exception classes set to true) and reuse across copies of the outbound flowIdeal for multi-unit tenants where each unit needs its own profile
Scenario-specific ProfileSet SkipRetriesProfile on the Partner ID that represents the scenarioOverrides global settings just for that scenarioWhen certain scenarios need stricter or looser retry rules

Examples

Example 1: Mapping Errors

Symptoms:

  • Messages fail with mapping exceptions.
  • Reprocessing without changing the mapping yields the same error. Messages can't be edited directly as they could be done in SAP Process Orchestration.
Setup:
  • Keep SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
  • Keep SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Result:
  • Mapping exceptions (com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException) go straight to DLQ.
  • The Integration/Operations teams can focus on either fixing the mapping or fixing the functional data.

Example 2: Receiver Not Found

Scenario:

  • You enforce strict routing rules—if a receiver cannot be determined and “Receiver Not Found” is set to Error, there is no fallback.
Result:
  • Integrated messaging runtime already skips retries and puts the failure in DLQ. No extra config required.

Example 3: Scenario-based Retry Profiles

Attach profiles to scenarios so each scenario can apply its own skip behavior without duplicating flows.

Scenario:

  • Scenario A (“Order-to-Cash Outbound”): skip retries for mapping errors only.
  • Scenario B (“Billing to CRM”): skip retries for mapping errors and known and expected script failures
Setup (Partner Directory profiles):
  • Create profile “SkipProfile_Scenario_O2C”: set com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException = true.
  • Create profile “SkipProfile_Scenario_BillingCRM”: set com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException = true and javax.script.ScriptException = true.
  • On the Partner ID for “Order-to-Cash Outbound”:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_O2C
  • On the Partner ID for “Billing to CRM Sync”:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_BillingCRM
Result:
  • Scenario A (“Order-to-Cash Outbound”) skips only mapping exceptions and retries everything else as usual.
  • Scenario B (“Billing to CRM”) skips mapping and javax.script.ScriptException, while still retrying potentially transient connection issues (for example, timeouts).

Example 4: A Single Scenario That Needs Stricter Skips

Sometimes one scenario needs stricter rules than the rest. A classic case: SOAP faults on partner onboarding, where the fault usually means the payload or contract is wrong and no amount of retrying is going to change that.
Scenario:
  • “Partner Onboarding (SOAP)” must never retry on SOAP faults because these typically point to permanent contract or payload issues.
Setup:
  • Create a skip-retry profile "SkipProfile_Scenario_OnboardingStrict" with org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault = true.
  • In the Partner ID representing this scenario, add:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_OnboardingStrict
Result:
  • Only this scenario overrides the global policy and skips org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault.

Configuration Options and Behavior

1) Default (Hard-coded)

  • In your Generic Outbound Processing flow:
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Behavior: Only mapping exceptions skip retry (class com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException).

2) Global Value Mapping

  • Set SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping.
  • Prerequisite: Deploy “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration” from the “Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates” package.
  • Default behavior of the value mapping:
    • com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException → true
    • All other exceptions → false
  • To add/adjust behavior, edit values in the value mapping and redeploy.
When to choose: You want one centralized table to govern skip behavior across the tenant. Note you can't deploy a copy of the provided value mapping with the same combination of agency and scheme, choose this option if you like to customize the skip retry handling globally across all scenarios.

 

3) Global Partner ID Profile (via Partner Directory)

  • Create a Partner ID that acts as your global skip-retry profile.
  • Add one string parameter per exception class to skip:
    • Parameter ID = fully qualified exception class name
    • Value = true
  • In your Generic Outbound flow:
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_SourcePidProfile = <YourProfileName>
You can quickly bootstrap this profile from the value mapping using the helper iFlow (below).

 

Helper iFlow: Auto-generate a Profile From the Value Mapping

  • Use “Pipeline v2 - Partner Directory Upload Helper - Skip Retry Configuration.”
  • Prerequisites:
    • In SAP BTP Cockpit Create a Service Instance for “SAP Process Integration Runtime” with plan apias described on SAP Help Portal and a corresponding service key. The required role for the service instance is AuthGroup_TenantPartnerDirectoryConfigurator.
  • Configure and deploy with:
    • API_URL = tenant-specific OData API URL (from your service key)
    • API_Credential_Alias = alias of the OAuth2 Client Credentials
    • PartnerDirectory_PID = target profile name (default SAP_Integration_Suite_SkipRetryGlobalProfile)
    • ValueMapping_TechnicalID = com.sap.integration.cloud.pipeline.v2.value.mapping.skipretry (default)
    • ValueMapping_Version = 1.0.1 (default)
The helper runs once per deployment, creates the Partner Directory profile, reads the value mapping, and writes exception class parameters with true/false. You can fine-tune afterward.

 

4) Scenario-specific Profile (Overrides Global)

  • First, create a skip-retry profile in Partner Directory (as above).
  • In the Partner ID representing your scenario, add:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = <YourScenarioProfile>
This setting overrides any global policy for that scenario.

 

Points to consider

  • Don't over-skip:
    • Timeouts, throttling, HTTP 5xx from backends usually resolve themselves on the next attempt. If you put these exception classes in the skip list, you’ll create incidents that would have healed on their own.
  • Copying value mappings incorrectly:
    • You can’t deploy a copy with the same agency/scheme. If you need variety across teams, prefer Partner Directory profiles.
  • Forgetting scenario overrides:
    • If a SkipRetriesProfile is set on a scenario’s Partner ID, it overrides global behavior—document and audit these.
  • Nobody's watching the DLQ
    • Skipping retries only helps if someone watches and acts on the DLQ. Ensure processes for analysis, ticketing, and replay are in place. If you’d rather park these messages somewhere other than the DLQ, take a look at the Process Integration Pipeline Extension - Restart via Data Store Community Package that allows you to store the messages in Data Stores. There’s a separate blog post covering it.

Configuration Summary

Global default (hard-coded):
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Global via Value Mapping:
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping
Global via Partner Directory:
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory
SkipRetriesGlobal_SourcePidProfile = SAP_Integration_Suite_SkipRetryGlobalProfile // or your profile
Scenario-specific override (on the scenario’s Partner ID):
SkipRetriesProfile = <YourScenarioProfile>

FAQs

  • What happens to skipped messages?
    • They go straight to the Dead Letter Queue (DLQ), where you can analyze and replay after fixing the cause. These messages will have the Custom Status "RetrySkipped".
  • Can I combine global and scenario-specific settings?
    • Yes. The scenario-specific profile overrides the global setting for that scenario only.
  • What’s the fastest way to create a global profile?
    • Use the helper iFlow “Pipeline v2 - Partner Directory Upload Helper - Skip Retry Configuration” to auto-generate a Partner Directory profile from the value mapping.
  • Do I need to copy the value mapping to change it?
    • No. Use the provided “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration” and adjust mapped values. If you need different configuration by unit or flow, use Partner Directory profiles.

Wrap-up

The "Skip Retry Handling" feature helps you avoid retrying messages that will never succeed. There are three ways to set it up:

  • Stick with the defaults: Mapping errors get skipped automatically. No additional configuration required.
  • Use Value Mapping: One place to control skip behavior across the whole tenant.
  • Use Partner Directory profiles: Best when different scenarios or teams need different rules.
Start with whatever’s closest to your setup and adjust as you learn which errors in your landscape actually need to be skipped.

entry.title: Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite 

This article explains how to migrate integration interfaces that rely on Dynamic Configuration (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Cloud Integration. It covers the technical root cause, a practical step-by-step migration approach, and the PO library dependencies involved. 

What Is ASMA (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes)? 

When running integration scenarios in SAP Process Orchestration, integration developers often need to pass dynamic, context-specific values — such as a target filename, a dynamic URL path, or a SOAP action — from the sender adapter through the integration logic to a receiver adapter. The mechanism that enables this in SAP PO is called Adapter-Specific Message Attributes (ASMA). 

ASMA values are carried in a special container called Dynamic Configuration, which travels with the message throughout the SAP PO pipeline. They can be read and written in three ways: 

  1. UDF in Message Mapping: In User-Defined Functions (UDFs) within a message mapping using the Java API: DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants.DYNAMIC_CONFIGURATION. 
  2. Java Mapping: In a standalone Java Mapping class that extends AbstractTransformation, using input.getDynamicConfiguration() to read or write ASMA values directly on the TransformationInput object. 
  3. XSLT Mapping: Via the SAP XI Mapping API extension functions provided by the SAP XML Toolkit. These extension functions allow an XSLT stylesheet to call into the Dynamic Configuration container to read and write ASMA values at runtime. 

Core API Dependencies for Dynamic Configuration 

Dynamic Configuration API depends on two specific JAR files that are part of the Process Orchestration runtime.  

JAR File 

Key Classes Provided 

Purpose 

com.sap.xpi.ib.mapping.lib.jar 

AbstractTransformation, TransformationInput, TransformationOutput, StreamTransformationException, AbstractTrace 

Core mapping framework. All Java Mappings must extend AbstractTransformation and implement the transform() method. 

com.sap.xi.mapping.tool.lib_api.jar 

DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants 

Dynamic Configuration API. Provides the typed container for reading and writing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes by namespace/attribute key. 

 

So, how to migrate an Interface with ASMA Using the SAP Integration Suite capability? 

1. Create a Data Extraction in Migration Assessment (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-assessment) 

Run Migration Assessment first to connect to your SAP PO system, extract all integration artifacts, and evaluate each scenario. As we will explore in this post, extracting this data reveals both the complexity and the use of Dynamic Configuration. 

Rules identified: 

  • GMMCustomUDFUsageCount 
  • GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguration 
  • JAVAMDynamicConfiguration 

 2. Migration Tooling (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-tooling?q=migration+tool) 

Using the assessment results as input, proceed with the Migration Tool for scenarios classified as "Ready to migrate" or "Adjustment required." The tool semi-automatically generates iFlows in SAP Integration Suite based on templates derived from the SAP PO configuration.  

3.Outcome & Validation 

After the migration, validate the migrated iFlows. The combined use of both tools ensures a structured, risk-reduced, and cost-efficient transition from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite. 

What Happens When You Migrate the Interfaces with ASMA 

Let's have a look at this demo scenario: 

 

1. Request Payload 

2. Processing Logic 

  1. Java mapping reads the input message, gets "action" and puts it into Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION". 
  2. XSLT Mapping converts the input into message into target format and store tag "message" into Dynamic Configuration attribute "inputMessage". 
  3. Receiver Communication Channel sets the Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION" into SOAPAction. 
  4. Once the target system processes the message, there is a Graphical Mapping with “User Defined Functions (UDF)”. This function assign values coming from Dynamic Configuration Attributes, "THeaderSOAPACTION" and "inputMessage", and puts into the tags "dynAction" and "inputMessage" respectivelly. 

 3. Response Payload 

 

Once the interface is migrated, you will find in your Integration Suite package the artifacts generated based on your SAP PO objects. 

Check the Interface (flow). You will see boxes representing the logic that was defined on your SAP PO with wrapper functions depending on your ASMA use case.  

 

JAVA MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) – A wrapper class is generated by the Migration tool by default to provide temporary compatibility. While you can override the Java method to retain Dynamic Configuration logic, this approach is not recommended for production use. The preferred approach is to replace the Dynamic Configuration calls with message headers as described in the adaptation section below. 

 XSLT MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - The migrated XSLT artifact retains the SAP XI Mapping API calls. These must be adapted to use Cloud Integration-native header access mechanisms. 

GRAPHICAL MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - UDFs referencing the Dynamic Configuration API are migrated as-is and will require manual adaptation. 

 

 

How to Adapt ASMA-Based Interfaces for Cloud Integration 

Evaluate your scenario — Identify all ASMA usages across Java Mappings, UDFs, and XSLT stylesheets. The Migration tool does not replace Dynamic Configuration logic. 

Understand how messages are processed by Cloud Integration. "Dynamic Values” are passed via named message headers or properties (set by a Content Modifier or Groovy Script step) not via a Dynamic Configuration container. 

 

 In SAP Cloud Integration, the correct mechanism for passing dynamic values through an integration flow is via message headers or exchange properties. These are set in dedicated flow steps before the mapping executes and then referenced in the mapping logic or adapter configuration. 

 

Concept 

SAP PO (Examples) 

SAP Cloud Integration (Examples) 

Dynamic value container 

  • Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) 
  • Message Headers 
  • Exchange Properties 

How values are set 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.put() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Groovy Script setProperty() / setHeaders() 

How values are read in mapping 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.get() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Graphical Mapping out-of-the-box functions to getProperty and getHeader 
  • Groovy Script getProperty() / getHeaders() 

Dynamic adapter config 

  • Adapter reads ASMA by namespace + attribute key 
  • Adapter uses expression ${header.HEADER_NAME} or ${property.PROPERTY_NAME} 

 

Therefore, if an imported mapping uses Dynamic Configuration APIs for accessing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, the integration flow must be manually adjusted to ensure correct execution.  

 

References 

Links 

Creating Message Mapping as an Artifact 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/developing-message-mapping-as-artifact?locale=en-US 

Headers and Exchange Properties Provided by the Integration Framework 

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/headers-and-exchange-properties-provided-by-integration-framework 

Access Headers and Properties in Scripts 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/access-headers-and-properties-in-scripts 

 

 

Let's Review the Integration Flow Imported in the Previous Steps and Look into Adaptations 

 

 1. Java Mapping Replacement: A Content Modifier step is added before the mapping to set the SOAPAction message header and store the inputMessage value into message property. This replaces the Dynamic Configuration to write operations previously performed in Java Mapping. 

 

 2. XSLT Mapping: Remove Dynamic Configuration functionalities 

 

 3. Graphical Mapping: UDFs adapted to use out-of-the-box functions instead of the Dynamic Configuration API. 

 

 

 

 Additionally, for XI Adapter, Natively Supports ASMA 

The XI adapter in Cloud Integration natively supports Adapter-Specific Message Attributes. The XI sender adapter automatically creates a pair of exchange properties for each ASMA: one holding the value and one holding the namespace. When configuring ASMAs for the XI receiver adapter, both properties must be explicitly created. 

For scenarios using the XI adapter, the migration of ASMA-based logic can therefore be automated. For other adapter types, a similar approach applies, with the key distinction that the namespace property is typically not required. It is specifically needed by the XI receiver adapter, but not by other adapters. 

In pipeline-based integration approaches this is handled automatically within the generic XI inbound and XI outbound flows. Since exchange properties are not passed between flows, corresponding headers are created at the XI inbound step and then converted back to properties at the XI outbound step. 

For further details, see: 

References 

Links 

Generic Inbound and Outbound 

Generic Inbound and Outbound | SAP Help Portal 

 

Summary 

Migrating integration scenarios that use Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite requires a multi-step approach. 

The recommended migration path combines two capabilities of SAP Integration Suite: Migration Assessment, to identify all affected integration scenarios and quantify ASMA usage through rules such as GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguratiom, GMMCustomUDFUsageCount and JAVAMDynamicConfiguration; and the Migration Tool, to semi-automatically generate iFlows from existing SAP PO artifacts. 

However, automated migration alone may not be sufficient. Depending on how Dynamic Configuration was used, each mapping type may requires targeted manual adaptation. 

By combining the structured assessment and tooling capabilities with these targeted adaptations, teams can achieve a controlled, risk-reduced migration from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite while preserving the intended runtime behavior of each interface.

entry.title: How to Enable Checkboxes for Configuration Parameters in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction:

In this blog, I will first demonstrate how to control payload logging using an externalized parameter with true/false values. Then, I will replace the text-based parameter with a checkbox and show how to achieve the same functionality in a more intuitive and user-friendly way.

 

Part 1: Using a Text-Based Externalized Parameter to Control Payload Logging

In the example below, I have added a Content Modifier to declare the Exchange Property enableLog, which will be used to control payload logging. 

The enableLog parameter has been externalized with a default value of true.

Next, I use the enableLog parameter in the Groovy script to determine whether the payload should be logged. Based on the configured value (true or false), the script either logs the payload or skips the logging step.

Important: Although the externalized parameter is configured with the values true or false, SAP Cloud Integration passes the value to the Groovy script as a String, not as a Boolean. Therefore, you should explicitly compare the value of enableLog with the string "true" (or "false"). This comparison returns a Boolean result, which can then be used directly in the if condition to control the logging logic.

I configured the externalized parameter enableLog with the value true. Since the condition evaluated to true, the Groovy script logged the payload successfully, as shown in the example below.

Part 2: Using a Checkbox-Based Externalized Parameter to Control Payload Logging

To follow along with this example, download the artifacts and extract the ZIP file. After extracting it, navigate to the following directory:

CheckBox\src\main\resources

This folder contains the files that need to be modified to enable checkbox-based externalized parameters.

Modify the parameter type from string to boolean, as shown below. This change instructs SAP Cloud Integration to render the externalized parameter as a checkbox instead of a text field.

Zip the file as below:

Upload the Zip file as below: 

Now you will get the checkbox as below.

Conclusion: 

In this blog, we explored two approaches for controlling payload logging in SAP Cloud Integration using externalized parameters. We first implemented the traditional approach with a text-based parameter that accepts true or false values. We then enhanced the solution by changing the parameter type from String to Boolean, which automatically renders it as a checkbox in the configuration.

Using a checkbox provides a cleaner and more intuitive user experience, eliminating the need to manually enter true or false values and reducing the risk of configuration errors. This simple enhancement can be applied to any Boolean configuration parameter in your integration flows, making them easier to configure and maintain.

entry.title: From Code to Strategy: How MCP, CPI, and Lightweight Agents Unlock AI‑Driven SAP Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) allows enterprises to connect diverse systems through iFlows — integration flows that define how data moves and transforms between endpoints. By combining CPI with the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and a lightweight Agent, we can expose SAP processes as consumable services — enabling seamless orchestration, automation, and intelligent interaction across enterprise systems.

 

Understanding the Components

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)

SAP CPI is the integration backbone of the SAP ecosystem. It enables secure, scalable, and real-time data exchange between SAP and non-SAP systems. In this architecture, CPI acts as the execution layer, where business logic resides. The iFlow processes requests coming from the MCP Server, performs necessary transformations, and interacts with SAP backend systems such as S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, or Finance modules.

In the AI world: CPI provides the structured data foundation that intelligent agents rely on. By exposing iFlows as APIs, it allows AI systems to access enterprise data securely and contextually — bridging traditional ERP with modern AI-driven automation.

 

Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server

The MCP Server is the middleware bridge between the Agent and SAP CPI. It defines “tools” — essentially API endpoints — that the Agent can call to trigger specific SAP processes. It handles authentication, routing, and error management, ensuring that requests from the Agent are properly formatted and securely transmitted to CPI.

In the AI world: MCP acts as the contextual translator between natural language and enterprise APIs. It enables AI agents to understand what business functions are available and how to invoke them, making enterprise systems conversational and intelligent.

 

Copilot Agent

The Agent is the frontline interface — a lightweight application (built with FastAPI or similar frameworks) that interacts with users or other systems. It consumes the MCP tools and provides a simple way to trigger SAP processes, either through a web UI or API calls.

In the AI world: The Agent represents the intelligence layer — capable of interpreting user intent, orchestrating workflows, and connecting to enterprise data through MCP and CPI. It’s the component that transforms static APIs into dynamic, AI-driven interactions.

 

Key Steps

1. Expose the iFlow in SAP CPI

  • Design and deploy your iFlow (e.g., invoice retrieval or employee data).

  • Ensure it’s accessible via HTTPS with proper authentication.

2. Create the MCP Server

  • Define tools mapping to CPI endpoints.

  • Configure the MCP server to expose these tools securely.

  • Example endpoint: http://localhost:8000/tools/sayHello

3. Consume with an Agent

  • Build a lightweight Agent using FastAPI.

  • Connect it to the MCP server and expose an endpoint like http://localhost:9000/agent/sayHello.

  • Provide a simple UI for users to trigger CPI processes.

 

Tools Used

  • SAP CPI → iFlow design and deployment

  • MCP Server → Tool definition and orchestration

  • FastAPI → Agent development

  • OAuth2 / SAP IAS → Authentication and security

 

Architecture Overview

Embed your architecture diagram here showing the flow: User → Copilot Agent → MCP Server → SAP CPI → SAP Backend Systems

Figure 1: End-to-end architecture connecting the Agent, MCP Server, and SAP CPI iFlow.

Step: Create a Simple iFlow in SAP CPI

Design a basic iFlow in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) that accepts a name as input payload and returns a greeting message. For example, if the payload contains "Dibyajyoti", the iFlow should respond with "Hello Dibyajyoti".

Once the development is complete, deploy the iFlow to generate the endpoint URL. This URL will be used later by the MCP Server and Copilot Agent to invoke the iFlow and integrate it into the overall architecture.

"https://<SUBACCOUNT>.it-cpitrial06-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http/hello"

Step 2: Test the iFlow Endpoint in Postman

Once the iFlow is deployed in SAP CPI, you’ll receive an endpoint URL. To validate it, use Postman:

  1. Open Postman and create a new request.

  2. Set the method to POST (or GET, depending on your iFlow design).

  3. Enter the endpoint URL generated during deployment (e.g., https://cpi.example.com/http/endpoint/sayHello).

  4. Authentication:

    • Username → Client ID

    • Password → Client Secret These credentials are provided when you configure the CPI tenant and are required for secure access.

  5. Payload:

    • In the request body, pass the input JSON or text payload (e.g., { "name": "Dibyajyoti" }).

  6. Send the request and verify the response.

    • Expected output: Hello Dibyajyoti

 

Step 3: Set Up the MCP Environment

Before working with the MCP Server, ensure your development environment is ready. You’ll need the following installed on your system:

  • Python (version 3.9 or above recommended)

  • PIP (Python package manager)

  • Visual Studio Code (for editing and running your project)


Step 4: Create the Project Folder Structure

To organize your MCP setup, create the following folders:

  1. Main Project Folder

    • Name: MCPPROJECT

  2. Subfolders inside MCPPROJECT

    • SAP_MCP_SERVER → Contains the MCP Server files (server.py, .env, requirements.txt)

    • COPILOT_AGENT → Contains the Agent files (agent.py, templates/index.html, requirements.txt)

 

SAP_MCP_SERVER

.env---->

requirements.txt---->

server.py---->

COPILOT_AGENT

requirements.txt------>

agent.py------->

create a subfolder

index.html--------->

 

Step 5: Run the MCP Server and Copilot Agent

Inside sap_mcp_server

Install dependencies and start the MCP Server:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs all required Python packages.

  • uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000 → Launches the MCP Server on port 8000 with auto-reload enabled.

Inside copilot_agent

Install dependencies and start the Copilot Agent:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000
​

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs Agent dependencies.

  • uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000 → Launches the Agent on port 9000 with auto-reload enabled.

Test the Agent

Once both services are running, open the Agent URL in your browser:

http://localhost:9000/

 This will load the Agent’s web interface, allowing you to trigger the MCP tools and ultimately invoke the SAP CPI iFlow.

 

Conclusion

By exposing SAP CPI iFlows through an MCP Server and consuming them with an Agent, enterprises can create a modular, API-driven integration layer. This approach simplifies access to SAP processes, improves reusability, and lays the foundation for AI-powered enterprise automation — where intelligent agents can interact with SAP systems naturally, securely, and efficiently.

Regards,

Dibyajyoti Nanda

https://www.linkedin.com/in/dibyajyoti-nanda-23907429/

entry.title: Implementing Dual Schedulers in a Single SAP Integration Suite iFlow: Supporting Ad Hoc and Schedule
entry.content:
Recently, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) introduced support for multiple Integration Processes in a single iFlow, enabling scenarios like separate scheduled and ad hoc execution paths.

The iFlow below uses two schedulers. The first scheduler is dedicated to ad hoc execution, while the second scheduler handles the regular scheduled execution.

Case 1: Normal Schedule

The iFlow runs as per the configured schedule using the regular scheduler.

 

Case 2: Ad Hoc Run and Automatic Switch Back to Normal Schedule

The iFlow can be triggered for an ad hoc run without modifying the regular scheduler. After the ad hoc execution is completed, the iFlow continues with the normal scheduled run automatically. Ad hoc run happen at 9.12. and as you can see it automatically trgerring every 5 min.

 

 

Conclusion

Using two schedulers in a single SAP CPI iFlow provides a reliable way to handle both ad hoc and scheduled executions. It eliminates manual scheduler changes, reduces operational risks, and ensures that regular scheduled processing continues without interruption.

entry.title: Integration Suite: Building an MCP Server for a SAP CI Monitoring Assistant
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Integration Suite, Message Processing Logs (MPL) are the primary tool for monitoring and troubleshooting interfaces. Whenever something fails, the same routine follows: log into the tenant, open Monitor, filter by artifact, drill into the failed message, read the error. In my previous blogs I looked at ways to make this easier — automating email alerts for failed interfaces, logging custom header properties for better MPL search, and storing logs in Database for long-term retention.

Now we are taking it a step further. SAP recently released the MCP Server capability in Integration Suite, which lets you expose any API as a set of tools that an AI agent can discover and call at runtime.
